title: Chapter 19: The Illusion of the White Hall
paragraphs: An elderly elf in a white robe embroidered with silver runes descended to the group of outcasts. He did not look at them with contempt—in his eyes, there was a wary, cold assessment.

"The High Elders have been watching you, strangers," the messenger said, shifting his gaze from the weeping lieutenant to the bloodied Yashil. "And they are intrigued by your... anomaly. The White Hall requires your presence. Follow me. And pray to the powers of the universe that your words prove as strong as your patience."

The path through Agdash was like ascending the steps of a giant mausoleum. Behind the Great Gates, there was no salvation—only a slow, deferred agony. On the lower tiers of the city, refugees huddles: haggard elves with extinguished eyes, dwarves who had lost their clans, and transparent Hydrols whose once bright neon glow had faded to the color of turbid, stagnant water. They watched the four strangers with empty gazes. No one threw stones. Hate, too, requires energy, and here reigned only an absolute, soul-sucking hunger.

Yashil walked steadily, ignoring the pulsing pain in his gashed cheek. Green drops of his blood fell onto the gray stone of the pavement, leaving behind a tiny, almost invisible trail of life in the realm of ash.

Kirah stepped with painstaking caution. Each of his steps was accompanied by a dull, threatening scrape of claws against stone. The four-meter demon, created to trample entire kingdoms into the mud, now looked like a huge, terrified dog that had accidentally wandered into a crystal shop. He instinctively tucked his head into his shoulders, trying to appear smaller, and tried not to look at the emaciated children hiding in the shadows of the alleys. The inner darkness whispered to him that he was the cause of their suffering.

They passed smithies smelling of ozone and ascended the broad basalt steps to the inner palace. The higher they climbed, the cleaner the air became. The pungent smell of the wastes was replaced by the scent of incense and old pine needles.
Finally, the guide in the white robe stopped before high double doors of solid moonstone. With a quiet, perfect rustle, they opened, admitting the outcasts into the White Hall.

The transition was striking. After the gray, rotting gloom of Agdash, the White Hall was blinding. It was a colossal, impeccably clean space, carved from white marble and pale jade. Under the high dome, defying all laws of fading physics, floated spheres of pure, warm light. The air here was fresh, cool, and absolutely dead in its sterility.

At the far end of the hall, on a crescent-shaped dais, sat seven High Elders. Their long, silver-embroidered robes flowed down the steps. Their faces, beautiful and cold as a glacier, expressed nothing but centuries-old arrogance. They were living ghosts of past greatness, desperately clinging to shards of their era.
As Kirah crossed the threshold, the flawless white marble beneath his claws emitted a quiet, protesting crunch and was covered by a network of fine cracks. The Elders flinched in unison.

"Stop where you stand, shards of a filthy world," the voice of High Elder Elarion rang out. It was smooth and devoid of emotion, like the ring of a crystal glass. "You desecrate this floor with every breath you take. And your blood, goblin, stains our stone."
Yashil stopped calmly in the center of the hall. Renée stood gracefully to his left, Janavar to his right, lazily leaning on the shaft of his axe, and Kirah loomed behind like a storm cloud.

"Blood is the only honest thing left in this world, Elder," Yashil said quietly but clearly. The echo of his voice resounded under the vaults of the hall. "Your guard at the gates spills it every day so that you can sit in this light."
Elarion narrowed his eyes.

"We were informed of your... performance at the Great Gates. You broke the will of one of our officers with a primitive psychological trick. A demon on a leash, a werewolf, and a harlot-fox. Did you come here to beg for food? Or to plead for a place in the barracks?"
Renée elegantly brought the mouthpiece to her lips. In the sterile air of the White Hall, the smell of her acrid tobacco seemed almost sacrilegious.

"Plead? Oh, spirits of the ancestors, pleading with you is too boring," the fox exhaled a stream of smoke directly toward the dais. "I look at these shining spheres under the ceiling, Elarion. Maintaining this pathetic illusion of past greatness takes so much mana that it could power the protective barriers of the lower ring for a month. Or purify water for those refugees below. But you prefer to sit in a beautiful, bright tomb. You are not rulers. You are corpses who are too stubborn to admit that you have already rotted."
One of the elders jumped up from his seat, his face flushing with red spots:
"Shut your filthy maw, beast-kin! You are in the heart of the most ancient civilization!"
Janavar yawned loudly and with relish, demonstrating to the elders his pink maw and a row of razor-sharp fangs.

"You know, we had a saying in the forest: a wolf is weaker than a lion and a tiger... but a wolf doesn't perform in the circus," the werewolf scratched behind his ear with a claw. "You've put on quite a circus here, long-ears. You sit on thrones while your house is burning. We didn't come to ask. We came to see if there was anything left in you besides pride. Spoiler: there isn't. Auf."

The air in the hall grew noticeably colder. The Elders' magic began to thicken, ready to descend upon the insolent ones. Kirah growled, dropping to all fours; his crimson veins pulsed faster, preparing to unleash destruction.
"Kirah. Janavar. Renée. Enough," Yashil's voice was not loud, but it carried such deep, tired wisdom that even the elders froze for a moment.
The small goblin took a step forward, leaving behind another green drop.

"You fight the Void with weapons and pride, High Elves," Yashil said, looking straight into Elarion's eyes. "But the Void cannot be killed with a sword. It feels no pain. It feeds on the decay of the fabric of the universe. And the more you isolate yourselves from those suffering below, the faster your world rots from within. I didn't come for food. I came to tell you that we are ready to stand on your walls. A goblin, a demon, a werewolf, and a fox. We are ready to die for Agdash. Not because you are great. But because if we don't learn to die for one other, then we are no better than the creatures crawling out of the portals."

Yashil's words hung in the unnatural silence of the White Hall. They were simple, devoid of magic and complex rhetoric, but they struck the very core of that existential wound that the elves tried to hide behind white marble. To die for one another. In this world, everyone survived only at the expense of another.
Elarion slowly sank back onto his throne. In his eyes, centuries-old contempt struggled with a sudden, painful realization of the truth spoken by this insignificant being.

"Your words sound beautiful, goblin," the High Elder finally said, his voice tinged with old-age weariness. "But words do not stop the Reapers. And they do not change the fact that your race is filth, and your demon companion is a bomb that will explode within our own walls. We will not accept your sacrifice. Leave."
Yashil slowly closed his eyes. He had done everything he could. The world was truly too stubborn in its desire to die.

But before the outcasts could turn toward the doors, the temperature in the White Hall dropped sharply and unnaturally. A puff of steam escaped Yashil's mouth. The shining spheres under the dome flickered and dimmed, acquiring a deathly pale, lunar hue.
From the shadows behind the Elders, from some hidden side gallery, came slow, rhythmic claps.
Clap. Clap. Clap.
The sound was dry and cold. The Elders flinched and stepped aside like a flock of frightened birds.

Out of the gloom, stepping absolutely silently, came a tall, impeccably built man. He was dressed in a strict black tunic of an old cut, the collar of which propped up an aristocratically pale face. His eyes were the color of clotted blood, and his black hair was flawlessly styled. He did not radiate wild, animal strength like Janavar or Kirah. He radiated an absolute, mathematical, inevitable cold.

"Remarkable," Lord Valtor said, and his voice, quiet and insinuating, filled every corner of the vast hall. "For hundreds of years, I have listened to your pompous speeches, Elarion. But today... today, for the first time in a long time, I heard the voice of reason in this hall. And, ironically, it belongs to a goblin."
Valtor stopped at the edge of the dais, looked down at Yashil, and slightly, barely noticeably, bowed his head. It was a gesture of respect from a predator to one who proved spiritually stronger.

"I am Lord Valtor," the vampire introduced himself, revealing impeccably white fangs in a slight smile. "And unlike these honorable elders, I value those who understand the true price of immortality and death. Lay out your cards, green-skinned philosopher. I want to listen to you."
